coredata.dc:title: A novel AuNPs-based nanosensors for smart detection of NO with low concentration
coredata.dc:creator: Peng, Yumei
Qin, Lixia
Kang, Shi-Zhao
Li, Guodong
Li, Xiangqing
coredata.dc:description: A novel method for detection of nitric oxide (NO) was developed by the structural transition of oxidized cytochrome c (Cyt c III) on gold nanoparticles (AuNPs)/glycine (Gly)/graphene quantum dots (GQDs) composite using surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) spectroscopy. Importantly, the composite can be used as a good SERS substrate for the selective detection of trace NO based on the interaction of the Cyt c (III) and NO, and the structural transition of oxidized Cyt c III on AuNPs/Gly/GQDs composite. The NO concentration in real seawater sample was determined to be 7.28 × 10−8 M. Combined with the interaction of the Cyt c (III) and NO, and the structural transition of oxidized Cyt c (III) on AuNPs/Gly/GQDs composite, a novel AuNPs-based SERS probe for the selective detection of trace NO was constructed, and the detection limit for NO can reach about ~10−8 M.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gold nanoparticles
Graphene quantum dots
SERS
Oxidized cytochrome c
NO sensing

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) of AuNPs (a), AuNPs/Gly (b) and AuNPs/Gly/GQDs (c).
TEM images of AuNPs (a), AuNPs/Gly (b), and high-resolution TEM images of AuNPs/Gly/GQDs (c) and GQDs (d). The insets in (a) and (d): size distribution histograms for AuNPs and GQDs, respectively.

(A) Raman spectra of AuNPs/Gly/GQDs/Cyt c-NO (a ~ i) and the concentrations of NO were 0 (a), 1 × 10−8 M (b), 1 × 10−7 M (c), 5 × 10−7 M (d), 9 × 10−7 M (e), 1 × 10−6 M (f), 3 × 10−6 M (g), 5 × 10−6 M (h), 7 × 10−6 M (i), respectively. Plots of SERS intensities versus NO concentration based on the bands at 1583 cm−1 (B), 1632 cm−1 (C) and 1560 cm−1 (D), respectively.
Illustration of the preparation of AuNPs/Gly/GQDs/Cyt c (III)-NO probe for detection of NO.

head.ce:title: A novel AuNPs-based nanosensors for smart detection of NO with low concentration

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Center of Graphene Research, Shanghai Institute of Technology, 100 Haiquan Road, Shanghai 201418, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Center of Graphene Research, Shanghai Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel method for detection of nitric oxide (NO) was developed by the structural transition of oxidized cytochrome c (Cyt c III) on gold nanoparticles (AuNPs)/glycine (Gly)/graphene quantum dots (GQDs) composite using surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) spectroscopy. Importantly, the composite can be used as a good SERS substrate for the selective detection of trace NO based on the interaction of the Cyt c (III) and NO, and the structural transition of oxidized Cyt c III on AuNPs/Gly/GQDs composite. The NO concentration in real seawater sample was determined to be 7.28 × 10−8 M.

Combined with the interaction of the Cyt c (III) and NO, and the structural transition of oxidized Cyt c (III) on AuNPs/Gly/GQDs composite, a novel AuNPs-based SERS probe for the selective detection of trace NO was constructed, and the detection limit for NO can reach about ~10−8 M.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, some reports show that the nitric oxide (NO) can mediate the signaling pathways as a cellular messenger and plays an important role in many physiological processes [1], and the effects of NO depend on its concentration, spatial and temporal constraints of the cell microenvironment. Also, the toxicity of NO is attributed to its ability to bind to heme proteins, resulting a disruption of protein functions [2]. Cytochrome c (Cyt c) is a water-soluble heme protein and it plays a role in apoptosis and is subject to nitrosylation [3]. The cleavage of the Fe–Met80 bond is important in several processes including the release of Cyt c from mitochondria or increase of its peroxidase activity. Nitrosylation of Cyt c can preclude the reformation of the disrupted Fe–Met80 bond and is shown to occur during apoptosis [4]. A report shows that NO binding induces pronounced heme conformational changes in the six-coordinate Cyt c–NO complex which activates apoptosis through its binding to Cyt c [5], and so on. Although there have been a considerable number of probes reported for NO [6,7], it is a still challenge to determine NO by the heme conformational changes using surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS).

SERS has proven to be an attractive technique for sensitive, ultrafast, and nondestructive determination of low-concentration biological species [8]. It is well known that the preparation of a good substrate is crucial in SERS applications. Recently, many reports have focused on constructing nano-materials that are from various nanoparticles including gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) [9], silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) [10], and quantum dots (QDs) [11]. Particularly, AuNPs become one of the most representative metal nanoparticles, and many SERS substrates based on Au have fabricated for sensing and trace analysis [12,13]. Also, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are increasingly drawing the peoples’ attention due to their stable photoluminescence, good biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, and chemical inertia [14]. Thus, to fabricate the AuNPs/Gly/GQDs nanocomposite as SERS substrate, it will be promising for detection NO.

Herein, based on the interaction of the Cyt c (III) and NO, and the structural transition of Cyt c (III) on AuNPs/Gly/GQDs composite, a novel AuNPs/Gly/GQDs SERS probe are fabricated for sensitive and selective detection of NO.
The simple strategy used to prepare the SERS probe is illustrated in Scheme 1.
The materials, the preparation procedure and detail characterization of the AuNPs/Gly/GQDs and AuNPs/Gly/GQDs/Cyt c (III)-NO probe are described in Supporting Information.

As shown in Fig. 1a, the XPS peaks of Au 4f (83.07 eV, 87.02 eV) [15], Au 4d (334.77 eV, 352.86 eV), Au 4p (545.77 eV), C 1 s (284.12 eV) and O 1 s (531.15 eV) are clearly observed. The C 1 s and O 1 s come from the citrate ions. Also, the new emerging peak of N 1 s (401.37 eV) in AuNPs/Gly (b) may belong to the formed C–N band between the terminal amino groups of Gly and AuNPs, indicating that Gly molecules are anchored on the surface of AuNPs. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 1c, the oxygen content at 531.15 eV gradually increases from 29.72% (in AuNPs/Gly) to 32.46% (in AuNPs/Gly/GQDs), in that a corresponding increase in the content of carboxyl groups and hydroxyl groups in GQDs (Table S1). It suggests that GQDs are bonded on AuNPs/Gly surface. Besides, from Fig. 1a to Fig. 1c, the content of Au gradually decreases, which also indicates that the surface of AuNPs is covered by Gly and GQDs, respectively.

As shown in Fig. 2a, clearly, the spherical AuNPs are monodispersed, and the size is focused on ~ 52 nm. The images of AuNPs/Gly (Fig. 2b) show that AuNPs are coated with uniform transparent layers, indicating that Gly molecules are coated on the surface of AuNPs. Also, the HRTEM image of the AuNPs/Gly/GQDs (Fig. 2c) show that more GQDs (the size is focused on 2–3 nm) insert into the AuNPs/Gly or adhere to the surface of the AuNPs/Gly. It is indicated that the AuNPs/Gly/GQDs composite are successfully fabricated.

Also, the FT-IR spectra (Fig. S1) and the typical UV–vis absorption spectra of AuNPs/Gly/GQDs and AuNPs/Gly/GQDs/Cyt c-NO (Fig. S2) are investigated. Results indicate that the AuNPs/Gly/GQDs/Cyt c (III)-NO complex could be formed by electrostatic interaction between the carboxylic groups of GQDs in AuNPs/Gly/GQDs and the amino groups of Cyt c (III) in Cyt c (III)-NO.

Based on the strong interaction of Cyt c (III)-NO with AuNPs/Gly/GQDs composite [5], the structural transition of Cyt c (III) is studied by SERS. As well known, the γ21 band (561 cm−1) is a ruffling distortion marker for c-type heme [16]. Obviously, the band in the spectrum of ferric Cyt c (III) ( Fig. 3A, a) vanishes after binding with low concentration NO (1 × 10−7 M, Fig. 3A, c). Also, its adjacent bands at ν24 (596 cm−1) and ν48 (636 cm−1) almost vanish absolutely. Besides, an upshift of Raman band νC-S of the thioether heme–protein bond from 681 to 683 cm−1 are observed with its sharply decreasing intensity (Fig. 3A, d–i). We can conclude that a more relaxed heme conformation upon NO binding is formed following by the breaking of Cys residues on AuNPs/Gly/GQDs surface (Scheme 1) [17]. Moreover, the high frequencies of marker bands ν2, ν4, ν5, ν10, ν11, ν21 and ν29 in Cyt c (III) also experience variable upshifts upon NO binding (1578→1583 cm−1,1361→1367 cm−1, 1121→1124 cm−1, 1553→1560 cm−1 and 1632→1630 cm−1, 1310→1313 cm−1 and 1399→1402 cm−1, respectively) [5]. Results further confirms that a rather large change in electronic distribution for a low-spin six-coordinate ferric heme in Cyt c (III) upon the exchange of Met80→NO [18]. Thus, the strong interaction of Cyt c (III)-NO with AuNPs/Gly/GQDs induces the pronounced conformational changes of Cyt c (III), which could be an excellent way to detect NO by SERS.

Clearly, as shown in Fig. 3A, when the concentrations of NO increase from 1 × 10−7 M to 3 × 10−5 M (c ~ i), the Raman intensity of the band ν2 (1580–1583 cm−1) increases together with its downshift and eventually steady to 1580 cm−1 (Fig. 3A). Also, the intense and sharp Raman bands at 1632 cm−1 and 1560 cm−1 are assigned to ν10 and ν11, respectively, their relative intensity enhances gradually with the increasing concentration of NO (Figs. 3B, 3C and 3D), and the detection limit for NO can reach about ~10−8 M (Fig. 3A, b). Thus, with AuNPs/Gly/GQDs composite as SERS substrate, the determination of low concentration NO is realized by the structural transition of Cyt c (III).

In addition, the effects of interfering chemicals on the detection of NO are examined under the identical conditions (Fig. S3). Results indicate that no significant change is observed in the SERS response of Cyt c (III) on AuNPs/Gly/GQDs surface in the presence of each relevant substance, suggesting the AuNPs/Gly/GQDs/Cyt c probe is highly selective for NO. Moreover, the SERS intensities of the AuNPs/Gly/GQDs/Cyt c based on the bands at 1560 cm−1, 1580 cm−1 and 1632 cm−1 have no obvious changes within a week (Fig. S4), indicating that the AuNPs/Gly/GQDs/Cyt c-NO complex is with high stability for a long time. The possible explanation is due to the strong interaction of Cyt c (III)-NO and AuNPs/Gly/GQDs composite which prohibit the dissociation of NO from His–Fe (III)–NO.

Moreover, the NO concentration in seawater sample was determined by the AuNPs/Gly/GQDs nanocomposite based on the interaction of the Cyt c (III) and NO (Corresponding experimental method and the spectrum are supplied in Supporting information). As shown in Fig. S5, after the purging-trapping operation, the conformational change of Cyt c (III) is consistent with above results (Fig. 3A) when the trapping NO from seawater was transferred into the Cyt c (III) solution and interacted with the AuNPs/Gly/GQDs. The NO concentration in seawater from the Fisherman's wharf of Shanghai (longitude and latitude: 121.525638, 30.818121) is detected to be 7.28 × 10−8 M according to the fitted equation [y = 118ln(x-0.05)+1403, R2 = 0.9367] at 1580 cm−1 (Fig. 3B). This method is fast and easy to be handled, and thus it provides a new idea for investigating the distribution of NO in the ocean.

A novel method for detection of NO was constructed by the strong interaction of Cyt c (III) and NO with AuNPs/Gly/GQDs as SERS substrate. This probe showed excellent selectivity and high stability, which could be used not only a good platform for studying the interaction of Cyt c (III) and NO but also can realize the determination of NO with low concentration in real seawater samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (no. 21771125, 21301118 and 21305092), and the Innovation Fund of China Petroleum Science & Technology (no. 2107D-5007-0207).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2018.08.091.
